audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_7: The initial cluster correctly identified the convergence of device, card error, and velocity signals. SQL deep-dive revealed that these signals are not isolated but part of a coordinated effort involving multiple accounts sharing device fingerprints and IPs.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_1: The provided evidence was highly consistent, focusing on identity verification failures related to location and device anomalies. No sub-clustering was necessary as all instances pointed to a singular typology: Account Takeover.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_6: The initial cluster correctly identified the 'deposit-and-run' behavior. SQL deep-dives confirmed that this isn't just individual behavior but a series of collusive pairs. KMeans clustering at k=4 would likely separate these into the specific payment-method-sharing groups identified.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_4: The initial evidence suggested a single pattern, but SQL analysis revealed two distinct sub-groups: one international (FRA/RUS) and one regional (EGY). Both follow the same behavioral template: shared device -> deposit -> token trade -> rapid withdrawal.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_5: Initial evidence suggested a single behavioral pattern. SQL deep-dive confirmed two sub-groups (EGY and RUS/FRA) linked by shared infrastructure. Sub-cluster A (EGY) shows 100% overlap in IP and device, while Sub-cluster B (RUS/FRA) shows device and card fingerprint overlap.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_3: KMeans clustering was not required as the SQL deep-dive clearly separated the two clusters based on shared identifiers. The primary cluster is defined by the intersection of shared device, IP, and recipient, while the secondary cluster is only linked by device.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_2: Initial clustering identified a tight group of 2 accounts sharing both device and recipient identifiers. No further sub-clustering was needed as the behavior was perfectly uniform.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_8: The evidence provided was highly consistent across all clusters, showing a clear pattern of new accounts, minimal trading, and device/amount anomalies. Re-clustering was not necessary as the SQL deep-dive confirmed these disparate signals were linked to the same set of device fingerprints and shared recipients.
audit_20260212_043755_84e10854_cluster_0: The initial evidence pointed to identity red flags. SQL analysis confirmed these were not isolated incidents but clustered around specific device fingerprints. No further KMeans clustering was required as the SQL JOINs on fingerprints provided clear, deterministic groupings.
